comments.text: The Wikipedia page on $p$-groups says that "there is a folklore conjecture asserting that almost all finite groups are 2-groups: the fraction of isomorphism classes of 2-groups among isomorphism classes of groups of order at most n is thought to tend to 1 as n tends to infinity". [Random numerology: There are $14$ groups of order $16$ and also $14$ groups of order in $(8,16)$. Similarly, there are $51$ groups of order $32$ and also $51$ groups of order in $(16,32)$.]

Thanks! I've just found some more background and results on this problem, which you might be interested in - added to the remarks above. The reference to Pantelidakis was found in [BNV07], but I haven't managed to find an online copy of the original DPhil thesis to double check this. Assuming this result is reported accurately, the conjecture for all odd $n$ is reduced to a finite check, which perhaps may be in range of some clever modern computations? EDIT: For more numerology, a list of $g(n)$ for $n\leq 2000$ can be found on the last couple pages of this report by Besche, Eick, and O'Brien.

Thanks. My numerology was in the direction of "Question 22.18 of [BNV07]", except I wanted it true for all $m$. So one version might be that $\sum_{2^{m-1}<n<2^m} g(n) \le g(2^m)$, which is true for small $m$ (and tight for $m=4$ and $m=5$). (This would imply the original Erdős conjecture above.) The table you mention is easy to look at as a human. For a computer, the OEIS has a file for $n\le 2047$ linked here. It's also available as FiniteGroupCount[n] in Mathematica.
